planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Mountain papaya requires male and female plants for fruit set — unless a hermaphrodite form is obtained. This species tolerates light frost better than common papaya.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; mountain papaya is a cool-climate relative of common papaya from the Andes of Colombia and Ecuador, adapted to elevations of 5000–10,000 feet where it tolerates light frost to 28°F — considerably more cold-hardy than the common papaya (Carica papaya). The small, yellow-green fruits are high in papain enzymes — even higher than common papaya — and must be cooked before eating, as the raw fruit is too enzyme-active to eat comfortably. Cooked, the flesh is used in jams, jellies, and stews throughout the Andes. In zone 9, mountain papaya can be grown outdoors year-round and provides a more climate-appropriate alternative to common papaya. The plant is dioecious; obtain plants of both sexes for fruiting.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; babaco is self-pollinating (parthenocarpic) and does not require a separate male plant. Protect from frost below 28°F.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 9–11; babaco is a naturally occurring hybrid of two Andean Vasconcellea species, selected and cultivated in Ecuador for its large, distinctively five-angled, torpedo-shaped fruits that are entirely seedless — a parthenocarpic trait that makes it the only papaya relative that does not require cross-pollination for fruit set. The flavor is refreshing and tropical, combining papaya with pineapple and strawberry notes, with lower enzyme activity than mountain papaya so the fruit can be eaten fresh. The tree stays compact at 6–10 feet and fruits prolifically in zone 9. In zone 9a, provide frost protection below 28°F or grow in large containers moved indoors in winter. A novelty fruit tree that produces a steady supply of unusual, edible fruit without the gender complications of other Vasconcellea species.
sources: New Zealand Institute for Plant and Food Research

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in lean, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; sheep sorrel self-sows freely and may spread aggressively in garden beds. In many gardens it is managed as a foraged weed rather than intentionally sown.

directions: Direct sow in lean, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; sheep sorrel is a diminutive native of Eurasia, now naturalized as a common weed across North America in sandy, acidic, infertile soils. The small, arrowhead-shaped leaves with backward-pointing lobes are more strongly acidic and citrusy than common sorrel (R. acetosa), with a bright lemon-sour flavor from oxalic acid. Young leaves are excellent in salads, soups, and sauces — the same culinary uses as garden sorrel — and the plant has a long history of use in traditional medicine as a spring tonic herb. Consume in moderation as with all oxalic-acid-containing plants. The plant spreads by both seed and underground rhizomes and becomes a persistent presence once established in appropriate acidic, lean soil; treat it as a semi-wild foraged herb rather than a crop requiring intensive management.

planting.note: Plant corms or divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; Chinese taro is one of the most cold-hardy alocasias and one of the very few that grows well in true shade conditions.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained to moist soil in partial to full shade in zones 8–11; Chinese taro is the most cold-hardy member of Alocasia and one of the few genuinely shade-tolerant large-leaved tropical plants, making it extraordinarily valuable for filling shaded spaces in zone 8–11 gardens with bold, architectural foliage. The waxy, medium-green, upright-pointing leaves on sturdy petioles have a distinctive hooded appearance (cucullata means 'hooded' in Latin). In Buddhist traditions across Southeast Asia, this plant is kept in temple gardens as a sacred plant and is associated with good fortune. In zone 9, it grows as a reliable outdoor perennial in shade, dying back in hard freezes and re-emerging from the corm in spring. The corms are technically edible after thorough cooking (raw alocasia contains calcium oxalate crystals), but this species is primarily ornamental rather than a food crop.

planting.note: Plant fresh cashew seeds (the actual nuts before roasting) 1 inch deep in well-drained, sandy, lean soil after last frost in zone 10–11; in zone 9, cashew is best grown as a container plant moved indoors in winter or in the warmest, most frost-protected microclimate available.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, acidic to neutral, infertile to average soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; cashew is native to northeastern Brazil and now cultivated throughout the tropics as one of the world's most important nut crops. The 'nut' is actually a large seed attached to the outside of a swollen, fleshy, pear-shaped 'cashew apple' (pseudofruit) which is edible fresh, juiced, or fermented into cashew wine. The seed shell contains urushiol — the same irritating compound in poison ivy — and must never be handled or roasted without protection. Commercial cashews are processed to remove this compound before the familiar pale nut is edible. The cashew apple, rarely seen in North America, is highly perishable and consumed fresh throughout tropical regions as a vitamin-C-rich refreshing fruit. In zone 9, treat as a container specimen moved indoors in winter.
